one.en: PARENTING – Where final parenting orders provided for the father to have sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with him, for a mo…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where final parenting orders provided for the father to have sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with him, for a moratorium of the mother’s time for 10 weeks, and for long term supervised time thereafter – Where the child left the father’s care and self-placed with the maternal family – Where the father sought a recovery order and injunctions against the mother and maternal family – Where the child was returned to the father’s care rendering the application for the recovery order otiose – Where the Court is satisfied that the maternal aunt had notice of the application but did not attend the hearing – Where the Court needs to take steps to protecting the integrity of its processes, to ensure compliance with its order and to protect the welfare of the child – Injunctions granted

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

By Initiating Application filed 27 January 2026, the father seeks a Recovery Order in relation to the parties’ child X born in 2013, as well as a series of injunctive orders.
In support of the application, he relies upon an affidavit sworn 27 January 2026. The Initiating Application and affidavit were directed to be served on 28 January 2026 with the mother to file and serve any Response and affidavit by 4.00 pm on 29 January 2026.
I am satisfied that the mother was served as was her sister, Ms Adir, who is identified in the Initiating Application as the second respondent. I am satisfied that the second respondent has notice of the orders that are proposed to be made against her.
The mother filed a Response that merely sought a dismissal of the Initiating Application. The Response was supported by an affidavit sworn by her and one sworn by her sister and one by her mother. The mother appeared but the Second Respondent did not. The mother said that her sister did not understand that she had to appear.

BACKGROUND

On 12 January 2026, I made orders and delivered reasons for judgment following a final hearing that occurred between 17 and 25 November 2025. The orders provided for the applicant father to have sole decision-making responsibility, for the child to live with him and commence spending time with the respondent on a supervised basis each fortnight, ten weeks after the making of the orders.
The reasons reveal relevant to this application, the following matters:
... I am not satisfied that the evidence establishes the father engaged in psychological abuse of the mother or that he perpetrated family violence on the mother.
Nor do I accept the submission that post-separation the father engaged in a pattern of aggressive, hostile and threatening behaviour. ...
...
In the context of the child’s asserted fears and the mother’s repeated assertion she has been subjected to family violence there is a disconnect between the child’s relationship with the father as reported by the father and his partner up until about mid-2024 and that reported by the child to the single expert, the mother, and the child’s treaters.

...
I do not accept the mother’s repeated assertions to numerous health professionals and others including the police that the child has been the victim of family violence perpetrated by the father. In that respect, I note Ms [M] reporting to the single expert the mother’s view that the child was being traumatised while Ms [M] and Ms [U] did not perceive there were any actual risk of harm issues in the father’s care.
I am not persuaded that the father engaged in coercive or controlling behaviour towards the child, or that he sexually or physically assaulted the child, or that the child has been the victim of family and domestic violence perpetrated by the father. I accept the opinion of the single expert expressed in paragraph 160 of her report that the child’s rejection of the father is influenced by enmeshment with the mother, loyalty binds, and the father’s lack of attunement.
I made findings that the father did not pose an unacceptable risk of harm to the child. In relation to the father’s assertion that the mother posed an unacceptable risk, I found the following:
The father contends the child is at an unacceptable risk in the care of the mother. I am satisfied that the evidence establishes as identified by the single expert at paragraph 169 of her report that the mother does not support a meaningful relationship between the child and the father. I am satisfied that the mother has undermined any attempt at ongoing therapy as made clear in the evidence of Ms [R]. The report by Ms [M] to the single expert makes it clear that the mother brings the child to therapy through ‘gritted teeth’. I am satisfied from the supervision reports that the mother has engaged the child in the litigation process and has used the child for her own purposes in the litigation.
...
In the primary care of the mother, the child is, as opined by the single expert, in an enmeshed environment that is psychologically harmful to her. The risks for the child moving forward are, as identified by the single expert, significant. They include reinforcing the child’s current distorted belief structure that the father poses a risk of harm to them and which may, as opined by the single expert at paragraph 168, cause long-term harm to their psychological well-being, their identity formation and their understanding of family relationships and risks permanently severing their relationship with the father and the extended paternal family. In the absence of a meaningful relationship, the expert opines that the child will grow up believing the father is abusive and harmful and without an opportunity to test or challenge the narrative would further entrench a distorted view of their family history.
I am satisfied that the above risks of harm are present and are continuing to occur and the magnitude of harm to the child is high. The mother presents no ameliorating circumstance that will reduce the risk of harm. Reducing the father’s time with the child is not an ameliorating circumstance but rather an exacerbating one. I am satisfied that leaving this child in the care of the mother is to expose the child to a continuing risk of harm which constitutes an unacceptable risk.
I recorded the child’s views and gave reasons in relation to them as follows:
I accept the single expert’s opinion as to the weight to be given to the child’s expressed views. As referred to earlier, I am satisfied that there is not a risk of harm to the child arising from the father’s conduct and/or behaviour towards the child. I find that the child’s stage of development “is still characterised by concrete thinking” and that the child does “not have a nuanced understanding of her family dynamics” nor does the child have “the maturity to comprehend the long-term implications of her expressed views” (Report, paragraph 157).
Ms [M] told the single expert that the mother has given the child a narrative and there was a need for the mother to direct the therapy. I accept that evidence. I also find that there is a strong enmeshment and the mother’s views and feelings influence and impact negatively on the child and impede their ability to have an independent view of the father and that it would be difficult for the child to express a view or consider an alternative view of the father different to that held by the mother. For these reasons, while I recognise the child holds these views, I do not attribute significant weight to them.
In relation to the matters under s 60CC(2)(d), I found:
I find the mother sees no value in the child having a relationship with the father and has actively undermined the child/father relationship. I do not accept the submission that the father’s pattern of interacting with the mother caused or created in the mother a reluctance to communicate with the father about parenting issues as addressed earlier.
I have no doubt that the mother deeply loves her child. The single expert described her as a caring, attentive, and fiercely protective parent. I have no doubt that the mother provides for the child’s practical needs. However, she is completely blinded when it comes to considering any benefit to the child in having a relationship with the father or any need on her part to encourage it.
The expert describes Ms [M] reporting the mother, whilst compliant with attending therapy sessions, brought the child through ‘gritted teeth’. The single expert expressed that as an unsurprising conclusion. The evidence of Ms [R], the court-ordered therapist, was that the mother, “is not interested or motivated to attend family therapy sessions nor to encourage the relationship between the daughter [X] and her father” (annexure to Ms [R’s] affidavit filed 28 October 2025, p.7). I accept that evidence. To that end, the mother has failed to engage in the therapy the court ordered.
I accept the opinion of the single expert where she observed the mother does not support a meaningful relationship between the child and the father and could not outline any significant role that the father could or should have in the future.
I am satisfied that at least from the end of 2023 if not earlier the mother was focussed on gathering evidence for the purposes of suspending time between the child and the father which culminated in her letter to the father dated 15 July 2024 (Exhibit 59). To that end, the mother arranged for the child to see therapists without the father’s knowledge and in breach of her parental responsibility obligations. The numerous attendances upon therapists and the encouraging of mandatory reports were done with the aim of ending the relationship. With the knowledge of what the child was reporting to her and the child’s declining mental health, including reports of alleged suicidal ideation, the mother failed to contact the father. I reject entirely any notion that her failure to do so was protective or because of a fear of his reaction. I find that to be an artifice confected to justify her behaviour. Her conduct in not informing the father exposed the child to a continuing risk of harm of which he was entirely unaware and done entirely to suit her own ends.
The continuing narrative the child is exposed to in the mother’s household is that the father is abusive and a risk of harm is destructive of and incompatible with the child’s psychological and emotional needs. The risk of harm is not borne out by the evidence. I accept the single expert’s evidence that the mother has imposed on the child a view that they are in danger which could have the effect of creating for the child a view that the world is a scary place and men are scary. I accept her opinion that this is detrimental for a child who already suffers some mental health difficulties. I also accept the evidence that this narrative of the father as abusive will impact on the child’s ability to form, manage and have positive relationships with others. If the child is to have any chance to grow and develop into a functioning adult able to form trusting relationships, the narrative needs to change.
There is no evidence that the mother has any capacity to change this narrative. Indeed, the evidence is that when the narrative is questioned, the mother disengages from the therapy as she did with Ms [M] and Ms [U].
The mother has engaged the child in evidence gathering for the purposes of the final hearing. The single expert described the extract in the supervision report where the supervisor reported the child saying they attended the supervised visits to ensure there was sufficient evidence as a “blatant example of intentionally involving the child for the purpose of gaining leverage” (Transcript 24 November 2025, p. 50, line 11–12). This conclusion accords with my own.
For the reasons referred to above and the evidence of the single expert, I am satisfied that the mother’s capacity to provide for the child’s psychological and emotional needs is so significantly compromised that she is unable to meet them.
...
I am satisfied that as between the father and the mother, the father has the greater capacity to provide for the child’s needs.
I identified the parties have no capacity to co-parent. In relation to what orders were in the child’s best interests, I found:
The proposals of the mother and the ICL do not meet what the single expert opines the child needs. Their respective proposals do not lead to disengagement from the conflict; indeed, they are highly likely to lead to more conflict and litigation with potential applications for contravention when, given the history of noncompliance with orders for supervised time, unsupervised time does not occur. They place the child in the primary care of a parent who has no interest in promoting a relationship with the other parent. They do not address the long-term psychological risks for the child, and they place the child with a primary carer who will only support engaging with health professionals who agree with her view of the father as the perpetrator of family violence and an abuser. The proposal is the antithesis of what is in the child’s best interest in exposing the child to an unacceptable risk of long-term harm.
The proposal of the father is one that on balance meets the requirements identified by the single expert. His proposal is more likely to lead to a disengagement from the conflict. It places the child in the primary care of a parent who is interested in promoting a relationship with the other parent. It addresses the long-term psychological risks for the child and places the child with a primary carer who will support engaging with health professionals to meet the child’s mental health needs.
I accept the evidence of the single expert that it is not too late for the child. The single expert in her oral evidence opined as follows:
And to your mind, the way – I beg your pardon – to your mind the balance of risk for [X] lies in favour of changing her residence, even though that might create a short term problems for her? --- We do need to balance those risks, don’t we, that – and I have noted that in my report that the risks are in the short term, she will experience distress in her father’s care. But then she may then settled. However, if she remains in her mother’s care, the status quo will remain. And then where does that leave her as a functional adolescent and adult moving forward with peers, with relationships, is my concern.

(Transcript 24 November 2025 p.63 lines 12–19)

...
The single expert recommended that the mother’s time will need to be supervised, and I accept that recommendation. The single expert said the following:
And it would only be after a period of supervision where the mother has essentially demonstrated that she can operate in a world when [X] lives with her dad?---Yes.

That then supervision might be lifted and something far more natural - - -?---Yes.

- - - might be able to happen?---Yes.

...

So does that mean that on the father’s proposal – or, no, let’s put it another way. Does that mean that if the court determined that she should live with her father, that time with her mother needs to be supervised?---That was the father’s proposal, and I think that - - -

No, no, no, I’m asking - - -?---Sorry.

- - - you - - -?---Yes, it does

- - - does that mean - - -?---Yes.

- - - does that – is that the necessary consequence - - -?---Yes.

- - - of a change of residence – is that the mother’s time should be supervised?---Yes.

And I presume, given that [X] has lived through this for the last decade or more, that it would be supervised indefinitely?---That would be – that would be necessary.

(Transcript 24 November 2025 p.42 lines 26–31; p.69 line 40 – p.70 line 9)
Given the expert evidence is that the mother’s time will need to be supervised indefinitely, I propose to make such an order conscious as I am that such an order is not one usually made. However, the circumstances and the expert evidence demand it.
The child came into the father’s care on 12 January 2026. The father’s affidavit records as follows:
That evening, we all had dinner together and I observed [X] smiling and engaging with us. It was really wonderful to have her home again. I observed her eating the food and she did not appear distressed or upset.
...
[X] also had her iPad and iPhone and I said to her “I would like you to give me your devices. You know you are not to have contact with mum” and she said “No. It's my property, you can’t take them”. I did not want to argue with [X] and said to her “I need to be able to trust you”.
From the outset, I took steps to stabilise [X’s] transition, including:
a) Engaging with [X’s] treating psychologist, Ms [M] on Wednesday 2l January at 10am for an initial session, and which was the first available appointment I was able to book;

b) Consulting with medical practitioners like.GP Dr. [Q] for a mental health plan, and getting referrals for [X] to start seeing their own female GP in the near future;

c) Planning school supports for the commencement of high school at [AA] School; and

d) Allowing her to have her mobile phone and iPad during the first week to communicate with friends, which I hoped would make the transition a bit easier.

...

On [...] January 2026, I again drove [X] to [BB recreation facility] at 2:00pm. I only saw her friend [K] this time, but [X] said the others would come along shortly. She asked me not hang around this time and go to a cafe nearby, insisting that “I’m not fragile and I’m not going to leave the [facility]”. Trying to rebuild the trust in our relationship, I agreed.

...
At 5:40pm on [...] January 2026, I reported [X] missing to NSW Police and attended [JJ] Police Station. While at the station, I received a call from [W] Hospital informing me that [X] had just been admitted there and they were about to do a mental health assessment.
I was not present when [X] was taken to the [W] Hospital, but I am informed by Hospital staff that [X] had contacted her mother's friend, [Ms B], who collected her from [BB recreation facility] sometime around 3:00pm and rather than contacting me, she took [X] to the Hospital.
When I arrived at the Hospital at 6:00pm, the Police were also present. I was interviewed by Constable [N] for 30 minutes about [X’s] disclosures of sexual abuse and other allegations. He told me he looked at the family case file before arriving at hospital and had some context about the situation. After the interview he informed me that he had no further concerns and would only file a mental health report and nothing further.
While at the Hospital, [X] was informing the Police, [DD Mental Health] staff, [EE Health Service], DCJ and the medical staff that I had sexually abused her when she was 2 years of age, that she was scared of me, that she did not want to come home with me. She was saying things similar to what the Respondent has historically asserted against me and had included in evidence against me in these proceedings. She also told staff about self-harming and suicidal ideation.
My fiancé [Ms E] and [F] arrived at the hospital at 6:30pm as I concluded the interview with the officers. We waited for hospital staff to conduct their assessment together with a hospital psychologist and counsellor. I had a conversation with the attending psychologist who had interviewed [X] for over an hour who told me “[X] has been making some vague allegations about sexual abuse that sounds very rehearsed. She used unusual language for children, but she is also threatening self harm. [Mr Kirill], I’m aware of the Court orders and the case history, but [X] is saying no one is listening to her and this might be a cry for help. But we still need to do further assessments but at this stage, we will be discharging [X] into your care this evening”.

...
At. 12:30am [Ms E], [F] and I left the ER with [X]. As we were walking outside [X] was very upset and began to shout at me. She said “If you truly loved me, you would just let me go!”, She demanded to be taken to her friend [D’s] house, the friend who had taken her to hospital with her mother. She also shouted, at me that “You have done so much damage to me my entire life”. I let her tell me how she felt and asked her if she wanted to sit down, which she did. After about an hour I asked [Ms E] and [F] to go home, after asking [X] if she was comfortable with that, despite repeating over and over that she did not feel safe with me or at our home, [X] responded that she was happy to stay with me.
At around 2:00am [Ms E] and [F] arrived home. [Ms E] sent me an image of the screenshot of [X’s] phone showing a long selection of notifications of missed calls and text messages. There were 9 messages from [X’s] aunt, [Ms Adir]; messages from the Respondent’s friend, [J] and other numbers...
[X] and I talked for three hours between 12:30am and 1:30am outside the ER. We sat on the bench in the dark, and she told me everything she felt, all her fears and anger and resentment towards me and what I had done to her and her mother. She did not want to hear my point of view and was quite sharp in telling me not to speak or to make it about me and just to listen, which I did. She said she felt better after telling me all these things, but that if I had really listened to her, I would take her to [D’s] house. At 3:30am I was very tired and told her that if she did not want to come home; I would have to admit her and she said “fine”.
At 1:30am we went back inside the Hospital ER. She was interviewed again by the attending nurse who took her to the Paediatric ward CJ was called once again. The DCJ case worker wanted to talk to [X], me and the attending nurse all on speaker together so we all went into a private room. She tried to understand what [X] was feeling, why she felt unsafe and why she didn't wait to go home with me [X] gave the same explanations but found it difficult to describe why she felt unsafe.
After about an hour, the DCJ case worker and nurse were quite adamant that [X] needed to go home with me, that that was the only option, but helped her compromise by suggested coping strategies like breathing, listening to music, reading or drawing to manage her anxiety. [X] threatened self-harm and to “kill herself” but the nurse and case worker ultimately decided to discharge [X] into my care. [X] and, I walked out of the hospital and went home around 5:30am on [...] January 2026...

(Reference to annexures omitted, emphasis as per the original)
The father says that he removed the child’s phone and iPad. He says in relation to that, the following:
Although [X] was upset and angered that I took away her phone and iPad, I noticed an immediate change as she was not receiving calls or text messages either from her mother or third parties.
The change in [X’s] demeanour that I observed included:
(a) reduced agitation;

(b) longer periods of calm,

(c) moments of spontaneous burnout, and

(d) increased tolerance of shared space with others.

(e) slept through the night once· settled,

(f) eating regularly when not emotionally distressed,

(g) Taken daily morning showers and managing their own routines,
(h) engaged frequent activities such as shopping; walking, watching television, listening to music, and reading,

(i) engaged in family dinners every night, sometimes contributing to conversation, smiling and even laughing.

(j) comply with household expectations when they are explained calmly,
(k) There have been no observations by me or [Ms E] of aggressive behaviour, threats toward others, or unsafe conduct within the home.
...
Between 12 January and 23 January 2026, I also observed the following:
(a) [X’s] interactions with [Ms E], [F], and [G] have been generally appropriate and, at times, warm. When spending time with [F] and [G] alone together, [X] tends to be more open and relaxed.

(b) [X] appeared more comfortable engaging alongside others rather than being the focus of attention, and was happy sitting in shared living spaces, watching television with [G] and [F], and participating in casual outings such as shopping or walks around the neighbourhood.

(c) [Ms E] has approached [X] with patience and emotional neutrality. [X] has responded to this by remaining present rather than withdrawing completely. [X] goes on walks with [Ms E] every morning for about 15 minutes.

(d) I have observed [X] laughing and relaxing in the presence of [F] and [G], particularly during low-demand activities, and has commented to DCJ they are funny (humorous).

(e) [X] has been making disclosures about self harming. I have observed that she was scratching her anus, which appeared superficial.

On Wednesday 21 January 2026 at 10:00am, [X] had her first session with [Ms M]. After her session, [Ms M] spoke to me briefly and said to me “[X] was engaged the whole time. She is frustrated at not having access to her devices which is making her feel very isolated.” We talked about the hospital visit on [...] and [Ms M] said to me “This was bound to happen, and I expect that she may run away, if not now, at some stage in the future. But [Mr Kirill], I'm really glad to see [X] attended today and she was engaged fully for the hour., talking to me about how she's feeling. She's happy to be going to [CC Health Service], so you need to continue with taking her there to speak to the counsellors. But what I'm seeing is that it's likely to get worse before it gets better”. I said to [Ms M] “How much worse can it get” and she said “Look [Mr Kirill], it's ok for [X] to test boundaries. That's expected of a resilient, intelligent, capable child like [X], but she's enmeshed with her mother’s narrative and your job now is to remain calm, loving and protective”.

(Emphasis as per the original)
He gave evidence of discussions with various officers of DCJ following a disclosure of an allegation of sexual abuse. The father gave evidence in that respect as follows:
[Ms E] and I waited for [X] at [GG shopping precinct] and received a call from [Ms S] and [Ms T], the case workers from DCJ who wanted to speak to us in person. We met with them for nearly two hours until 7pm. [Ms S] explained that “[X’s] disclosures in particular the sexual abuse allegations date back to 2016, and when we pressed [X] she can't remember any details but is still talking about. [X] claims to be constantly fearful and feels unsafe in yow· house. She complained that you are withholding food, that she does not have access to water and that you [Mr Kirill] have made comments about her weight and eating in the past. [X] also does not understand why you don’t know what her favourite foods are and that you have not asked her what she likes to eat now. She also is still very confused about the Orders and said that no one has explained them properly to her”. I said to [Ms S] and [Ms T] “Sure, I haven't asked [X] what her favourite food is; but she's been eating and laughing and engaging with us at dinner”. [Ms S] raised the issue of suicidal ideation but she was satisfied that there wasn’t anything further to investigate but she did want to help the family going forward, potentially suggesting family therapists for me and [X], as well as liaising with high school counsellors.
At the conclusion of the meeting with the DCJ officers, [Ms S] informed me “I will conclude our investigation with a home visit as I would like to speak to the entire family, including [F] and [G], but we can’t do that next week”, which we agreed to and then [Ms T] said to us “'[X] prefers not to involve the police, because she doesn't want to get you into trouble”'.
(Emphasis as per the original)
He says that on 24 January 2026, he woke up and discovered that the child was not in his home. He says that he called the child’s mother. In that respect, he says:
[Ms E] and I went to [KK] Police Station to report [X] missing. I informed the Police about the previous incident, and the Police attempted to contact [Ms B]. According to Constable [O], [Ms B’s] phone was disconnected. I texted the Respondent at 9:53am, and informed her that [X] had run off, and her response at 10:45am was “I have just had a call from the police. I have heard nothing about [X’s] whereabouts. Please keep me informed”. The Respondent's response made no sense to me as the Respondent is hypervigilant when it comes to [X] and for her to be calm and dismissive of the situation, was out of character.
The Respondent has not contacted me or made any further enquiries about her missing daughter since Saturday morning.
I was at the Police station when the Police confirmed that Ms [B] lived in [suburb NN] and that they would be going to her house. The Police also called the Respondent who informed. them that she was currently in [suburb NN]` “having a coffee with a friend” and that she had absolutely no idea where [X] was and had had no contact with her.
(Emphasis as per the original)
He thereafter says the following:
I was made aware of [X’s] whereabouts at 7:00pm on Saturday night, 2026, by a Senior Constable [P] in the [LL] Police [Station], that she had travelled a significant distance from my home and she was being kept safe by relatives. He would not tell me who and in fact, told me that [X] was safer with her relatives than with me as she was alleging that I had sexually abused her.
I was also informed by the Police Officer that [Ms Adir], [X’s] aunt took [X] to [V] Hospital that evening. I attempted to call [V] Hospital a number of times on 24 January 2026 but was told each time that no [X] had been admitted there.
[Ms Adir], nor any of the other relatives with whom [X] could be staying, has not contacted me to inform me of [X’s] whereabouts or her welfare.
Today, 27 January 2026, 1 telephoned [Y] School and spoke to a lady at the enrolment office who confirmed that [X] is enrolled at [Y] School and is due to commence in Term 1 2026. As l understand it, [Y] School is a selective school, and [X] would have had to sit the selective school exam sometime last year. l believe that the Respondent had an intention to relocate to the [city MM] area and has taken active steps to enrol [X] at this school. At no time during the Trial before the Honourable Justice Schonell did she inform the Court of this.
...
I firmly believe that the Respondent is aware of where [X] is, and has been in direct communication with [X]. I firmly believe that [Ms Adir] is aware of the Court Orders and is knowingly retaining [X] in contravention of the Court’s orders and facilitating contact with the Respondent mother.
The mother in her affidavit states that on 24 January 2026 she was contacted by her mother. Her affidavit records:
At around 7:00pm, I received a call from my mother saying that [X] had turned up at my niece’s doorstep. My niece then called Police to notify them of [X’s] location. Police subsequently interviewed [X] and ordered an ambulance after raising concern about the amount of self-harm marks on [X’s] body and after [X] provided them with a suicide note where they had documented their intention to kill themselves on [...] January 2026. [X] was admitted to [V] Hospital and the Police reportedly stayed the night with [X] after Police expressed concern that [Mr Kirill] would turn up at the hospital.
I understand that [X] received a psychiatric assessment and was released by the hospital’s Mental Health Care Team on [..] January 2026 into my sister [Ms Adir’s] care. I have had no contact with my sister since the morning of [...] January 2026 to ensure that I was not in breach of the Court Orders. I have also had no contact with [X].
...
On 27 January 2025, I received a call from staff at [Y] School informing me that [Mr Kirill] had contacted them asking them in [X] was enrolled to attend. I confirm that in 2024 [X] sat a selective high school entrance exam at her request. [X] hoped to pass the test and attend [Z] School, however, did not receive an offer. [X] was only accepted into [Y] School in December 2025 as a late acceptance after being waitlisted. I accepted the placement only to keep [X’s] options open. I held no immediate plans of relocating to [city MM] as my employment is in Sydney. In the event that the Court made the Orders I was seeking at the Final Hearing I intended to have a discussion with [Mr Kirill] about potential school options for [X] and if he agreed, I was prepared to transport back and forth from [city MM] to facilitate [X’s] time with [Mr Kirill].
I am greatly concerned that since transitioning into [Mr Kirill’s] care [X] has started self-harming again and has already been admitted into hospital on two occasions. I am concerned that [X’s] mental health is rapidly declining in [Mr Kirill’s] care and by a Recovery Order being granted, [X] is at significant risk.
[X] has a very close relationship with my sister who is also [X’s] godmother. My sister [Ms Adir] is a teacher and is in a stable and loving relationship with her husband [Mr C] who is also caring and involved with [X]. [Mr C] has two adult children who no longer live at home and my sister has three adult children.
Whilst I continue to hold significant concerns about [X’s] wellbeing and safety in [Mr Kirill’s] care, I have not and will not breach the Court Orders. I consider the option of [X] living in my sister’s care to be the best option in ensuring [X’s] safety and wellbeing is prioritised pending further Orders.
I do not agree with [Mr Kirill’s] Application that restraints be imposed upon myself, my sister, [Ms B] or any third party from contacting or accepting contact from [X]. It is my view that this is overly restrictive on [X] and it will negatively impact [X] to be further isolated from her family and network.
The maternal grandmother’s affidavit has no bearing whatsoever on the issues before the Court apart from making clear that the maternal grandmother did not consider that it was important the father be informed as to the child’s whereabouts. The mother’s sister’s affidavit records that she well understood what orders had been made by the Court. Her affidavit records as follows:
On Sunday, [...] January 2026, I attended [V] Hospital at approximately 7.00 a.m.
This was the first direct contact I had with [X] since September 2025.
I was spoken to by hospital medical staff and a social worker, who advised me that police had determined that [X] should not be returned to her father at that time due to concerns including suicidal ideation and evidence of self-harm.
I personally observed multiple relatively fresh cut marks on [X’s] left arm extending from her wrist to her shoulder.
I was advised that a report would be made to the Department of Communities and Justice (DCJ) and that further assessment would occur.
I informed hospital staff and the social worker that I understood Mr [Kirill] had sole parental responsibility under the new orders and that [X’s] mother was not permitted to have contact with her.
I also advised that I would not permit contact between [X] and her mother while [X] was in my care, which the social worker indicated was appropriate.
Hospital staff advised me that [X] stated she felt safe with me and that, based on their assessment, she would be discharged into my care on an interim basis.
I was advised that a [FF Health Service] referral would be made and that DCJ would contact me after the long weekend with further guidance.
...
On Tuesday, 28 January 2026, I was contacted by a JIRT caseworker named [Ms S who conducted a welfare check with [X] and advised me she would inform Mr [Kirill] that [X] was safe.
[Ms S] advised that she believed the court orders may restrict contact between [X] and maternal family members. I informed her that I had not seen the orders and was unaware of this.
Later that day, [Ms S] advised me that DCJ had assessed the matter and that the threshold for intervention had not been met, and that the court orders remained in effect.
I sought advice regarding how to manage [X’s] return to her father given her expressed distress and threats of self-harm. [Ms S] advised she would attempt to consult the court appointed psychologist.
On Wednesday, 29 January 2026, arrangements were made for [X] to be returned to her father.
Return of [X] to Her Father
On Thursday, 29 January 2026 at approximately 10.00 a.m., [X] was returned to Mr [Kirill] at [HH] Railway Station.

...
At no time prior to [X] leaving her father’s home did I have any knowledge of her intentions.
The discharge summary annexed to the mother’s sister’s affidavit relevantly records the following under the heading of ‘MSE’. It records “she”, being a reference to X, “was dressed according to weather, engaged with good eye contact”, “mood is getting better”, “she denies any suicidal thoughts and no plans of self harming”, and “reports she is happy to be her cousins and her cousins are happy for her to stay in their house”.
It later on records under the heading of ‘Assessment and Management’, “from a mental health perspective, X’s risk of self harm is low and appears reactionary”.

DISPOSITION

Consistent with the provisions of s 69ZL of the Act, I set out in short form my reasons.
In circumstances where the child has been returned to the care of the father, the father no longer seeks a recovery order but seeks various injunctions.
I have previously determined that it is in the best interests of the child that she live with the father. Apart from the effluxion of time, some 18 days, no new issue has arisen that would suggest there should be a reconsideration of that determination. The fact that the child has run away was contemplated as a risk in consideration of the making of the final orders as was concerns that she may self-harm. Likewise, the fact that the mother would not facilitate a relationship between the child and the father was also a factor in the considerations that led to the making of the Court’s orders.
The mother opposes the orders that are sought and says that she is concerned that if the orders are made, she is fearful the child may take her life.
The contents of the mother’s affidavit and her failure to advise the father of her knowledge of the child’s whereabouts including her unilateral actions in relation to the child’s schooling all reinforce the correctness of the Court’s decision and its conclusions in relation to her parenting capacity and the risk of harm she poses to her daughter.
There is no evidence before me that suggests that the factual assertions that underpinned my judgment have changed, nor is there any new fact or circumstance. The Court has made final orders that the child is to live with the father and to spend limited time after a moratorium period with the mother. There is no matter before me that suggests that those orders should in any way be reconsidered. Nor is there I note any application to do so.
It is concerning the mother took no steps to inform the father of what she knew. The fact that she has not done so speaks volumes as to her limited insight and her parenting capacity.
The evidence reveals that the child was in the care of Ms Adir with the mother’s implicit consent because the evidence reveals that she has taken no active steps to cause the child’s return to the father. In circumstances where the Court has determined that the child should be in the care of the father, but was in the care of a third party, then the Court needs to take steps to protecting the integrity of its processes, to ensure compliance with its order and to protect the welfare of the child.
I am satisfied that the circumstances of this matter are exceptional and that there is a basis established on the evidence to which I have earlier referred to proceed against Ms Adir. While she has been given notice of the orders that are sought and, in the circumstances, I intend to grant her liberty to apply on 48 hours written notice to my chambers to be heard as to whether orders should be made removing her as a party and/or relieved of the obligations contained in the orders I propose to make.
I propose to make orders as follows.
